title: Ribollita
subtitle: Thick Tuscan bread and bean soup with cavolo nero.
excerpt: Cannellini beans, cavolo nero, and a thick vegetable base simmered with stale bread until the soup becomes almost solid. Ribollita means 'reboiled': it is made the day before and reheated, which is when it is best. A Tuscan winter staple that is as much stew as soup.
sourceNotes: Ribollita is a Florentine and broadly Tuscan preparation, one of the most well-known examples of cucina povera cooking. It was historically made to use up leftover minestrone or bean soup by reboiling it with stale bread the following day, producing a thicker, denser dish. The canonical version includes cavolo nero, cannellini beans, and white bread; regional variations include tomatoes, potatoes, and various greens. It is listed in traditional Florentine cookery as a winter dish associated with the colder months.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to three months. Reheat with a splash of water — it thickens considerably on freezing.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well. Better the next day.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a day ahead and reheat. Ribollita ('reboiled') is the name given to the twice-cooked version, which is the point.

content.text: The bread is not a garnish. It goes into the soup and dissolves into it over long simmering, creating the characteristic thick, porridge-like consistency. Use stale white bread or ciabatta — fresh bread stays in clumps rather than absorbing evenly into the liquid.

Ribollita is better on the second day. Make it, refrigerate it, and reheat it the next day with a splash of water. The name refers to this second boiling: it is not a mistake that it has been left to sit.

items.prepNote: stale white bread or ciabatta, crusts removed

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large pot over a medium heat. Add the onion, carrot, and celery and cook for 10 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the tinned tomatoes, thyme, and bay leaves. Cook for 5 minutes.

Add the {{cannellini-beans}} of drained beans and {{stock-vegetable}} of stock. Bring to a simmer. Mash about a quarter of the beans against the side of the pot to thicken the soup. Add the cavolo nero and stale bread (torn into pieces). Stir well. Simmer for 30–35 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the bread has completely dissolved and the soup is very thick. Remove the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper.
Refrigerate overnight. The next day, add a splash of water and reheat gently, stirring to prevent sticking. Finish with a drizzle of good olive oil.

items.symptom: Soup is too thin after the bread dissolves
items.cause: Not enough bread, or too much stock.
items.fix: Add more torn stale bread and continue simmering. Ribollita should be thick enough to hold a spoon upright.

items.symptom: Cavolo nero is still tough after 30 minutes
items.cause: Stalks not removed, or leaves not shredded finely enough.
items.fix: Remove the tough central stalks completely. The shredded leaves should be soft after 20 minutes of simmering.

items.symptom: Soup has stuck and scorched on the bottom
items.cause: Heat too high once the bread dissolves and thickens.
items.fix: Reduce the heat to its lowest setting and stir frequently once the bread has been added. A heavy-based pot helps.

items.cause: Soffritto not cooked long enough, or beans under-seasoned.
items.fix: Cook the onion, carrot, and celery base for a full 10 minutes. Season generously — ribollita needs more salt than a thinner soup.

content.text: Ribollita belongs to the cucina povera tradition of Tuscan cooking: peasant food that used everything and wasted nothing. Stale bread thickened the leftover soup, making yesterday's dinner into today's more substantial meal. That structure, reusing and improving something already cooked, is a useful principle beyond this specific dish. Ribollita entered British cooking through the broader adoption of Italian regional cuisine in the 1980s and 1990s, partly via Elizabeth David and later through the influence of River Café cookbooks, which focused on the simple preparation of quality Tuscan ingredients.
